item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's good, a tad confusing but good. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They only found one Daevite, who had documents indicating they were on a mission to plant the seed. It seems quite likely they had gotten captured after/while planting the seed, and thus were trapped along with the rest while it "went off".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, doesn't the text imply that, at some point, SCP-4008 affected Daevites themselves, since they were found at the dig site?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Done

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since this item is now SCP-4008, I think in-text references to 4000 (like log items) might need to be changed accordingly. Sorry for nitpicking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ha, I guess maybe I'm biased, but I was initially disappointed by the final note, but then turned around again by its relation to SCP-1000. (Completely believable, too, in my opinion.)

Anyway, I really enjoyed reading this whole thing. I particularly like the in-progress article conceit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good shit. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Children of the Night is one of the names used to describe SCP-1000

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dig it (pun intended).

What are the Night's Children supposed to be?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The tie in to the new Department of Abnormalities SCP is wicked cool and I think this is made better for it. This whole article is awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well it certainly wouldn't have been done by the Daevites, but who knows how many of those seeds are out there or where they've gotten around to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, I really enjoyed this article.
Data corruption usually bothers me in that it often "conveniently" blots out important information, but I think you did it well here, especially given that we get a nice uncorrupted file at the very end.
+1

I wonder if something similar to this happened in Roanoke…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah it's not a literal reference, more a force that poisons the land. There are other reasons for the name too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the story, but what does this have to do with Wormwood?

Wormwood is described as a star that is supposed to poison one third of the world's rivers.

item.title: Re: Minor Edit
item.content:encoded: Because it's not censored data that is missing, the data is corrupted, so while you we could infer the next words are "to be" we can't say for sure because the actual record of those words is not readable.

item.title: Re: Minor Edit
item.content:encoded: But if it’s “there definitely used to be …” why would “to be” get censored? I’m too scared to read it myself (that’s how I ended up here, looking for declassified), so now I’m confused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, I appreciate you giving it another go!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah the footnote/crosslink isnt a bad idea. I'll see if I can find something appropriate to link in. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I slept on this earlier, mainly because I watched you brainstorm the article and thus knew the ending ahead of time. But the journey through the article was much more interesting than the destination - it's a fascinating take on the myths of lost cities and a clever extension of existing Daeva lore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like how your story takes its roots in those exploration stories, where ancient artifacts and civilzations are exhumed, yet branches out to a darker, more unsettling origin. And the prose feels really organic. I think this one is one of the most well-seeded for the competition.

Just one thing: I didn't know about the Daevites until I looked them up, so maybe a footnote or crosslink would reinforce the mental imagery. Though I suppose that's only me.

Other than that, +1.

item.title: Re: Minor Edit
item.content:encoded: Ah I see now, I was thinking used was in the context of having used in the past, not existing at some past time. Makes perfect sense now. Thanks for getting back to me.

item.title: Re: Minor Edit
item.content:encoded: "There" is correct - don't forget there's a chunk of missing information there. You aren't fully seeing the sentence that follows it.

item.title: Minor Edit
item.content:encoded:
Hey there, I'm not comfortable enough with the site yet to do minor edits, but wanted to bring to your attention:

Site Gamma, day 14. We've made some truly remarkable discoveries here. There definitely used [DATA CORRUPTION]

I think this should be 'They' if I'm not mistaken.

Awesome article btw! +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved this as a draft and I love it here, as it so far has really done the best job with the "History" theme of all the entries I have read so far.

You do a good job building up suspense as to the fate of Site-183, and I feel the big reveal is a good payoff for the wait. Having places like Atlantis, etc being the results of Daevite WMD warfare is quite creative and really adds a new level of complexity to the SCP Wiki's most famous lost civilization.

Whole-hearted +1, Mortos. Excellent work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure how I feel about the "actual anomaly being at the end" thing, but overall I think you pulled the whole thing off well, and the text beforehand significantly added to the payoff at the end. My only regret is not reading up enough on the Daevites before this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really enjoy this one and I think it fits nicely with the ancient / pre-historic lore so far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, that's exactly what I was going for, so I'm glad people are picking up on that. I originally had a slightly different concept where it actually did alter history itself rather than just peoples memories, but I thought this way was ultimately more interesting (and left more stuff behind to actually work with).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Y'know, I think this is the first time we've seen the Daeva combine their 're-writing history' and 'magic plants' schticks. It feels like a fairly obvious thing to do, in retrospect, and indeed this feels like the natural direction to take that concept.

But I mean that as praise; doing something that seems so obviously right in retrospect is no easy feat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For once, something major goes missing and unremembered, and the Foundation's like "could it be antimemes?" That's a nice change of pace.

Heh, yeah I figure they must learn eventually, so I figured laying out all the most reasonable possibilities would be something they'd do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For once, something major goes missing and unremembered, and the Foundation's like "could it be antimemes?" That's a nice change of pace.

Footnote four would be better off saying when the Site went missing in relation to the event being talked about (so, 'two days prior').

Neatly wrapped up by the end, but I don't think that's a bad thing. This tells a great story, and I like the way it's laid out. I even didn't mind the whole "Daevites did it" thing, for once. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot. You create this wonderfully empty, hollow world, then slowly reveal the cause in a pleasantly understated way. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, that makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
potential spoilers
– hide block

It wasn't that they planted it (which obviously would have been daft), but that breaking the crystal shell of the thing triggered it to go off, which they would have had no way of knowing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great. Articles with long logs are difficult to do well, due to the need to hold reader interest throughout all of the article, but this article does it well. It’s not my favorite entry, but it’s up there. Although I do have to wonder what moron thought planting these would be a good idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I adore this. The logs are the perfect amount of cryptic, and the realization of what this is at the end is mesmerizingly unsettling. I can't stop thinking about this article.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
I joined the SCP community in the closing days of the 3000 contest, and it's been a pretty wild ride. This is the 20th article I've posted in my time here. There have been ups and downs and my writing has been far better received that I could have imagined.

This is my SCP-4000 contest entry. I hope you like it.

Thanks to CaptainKirby, NineVolt, Conwell, DrChandra, and especially djkaktus for his advice on last minute additions and the idea for the title of this thing.

You can find more of my stuff here!

Image sources:
https://pxhere.com/en/photo/827766
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Jerycho8.jpg
